title: Undo and Redo
subtitle: {key:u} to undo. {key:Ctrl-R} to redo. You cannot lose work.
summary: u undoes the last change. Ctrl-R redoes it. Vim's undo is a tree, not a stack — you literally cannot lose work.

blocks.text: Vim is safe. Whatever you just did, you can undo. Whatever you just undid, you can redo. There is no "oh no" key — only {key:u}.
Press {key:u} repeatedly to undo further. Press {key:Ctrl-R} repeatedly to redo. Counts work too: {key:5}{key:u} undoes the last five changes.

What counts as "a change"? An entire Insert-mode session, from {key:i} to {key:Esc}, is one change. An operator+motion (like {key:d}{key:w}) is one change. A single {key:x} or {key:r} is one change. So {key:u} undoes the *last meaningful action*, not the last keystroke.

blocks.title: Vim's Undo Is a Tree
blocks.text:
In every other editor, undo is a stack. Undo pops; redo pushes. If you undo three steps and then make a new edit, the redo stack is destroyed — those three changes are gone forever.

Vim's undo is a *tree*. Every change creates a new node. Undo walks toward the root. Redo walks back the way you came. **Branching changes do not destroy history**: when you undo three steps and then make a new edit, you create a new branch from that point. The old branch is still there, and Vim can walk to it.

The practical consequences:

  • {key:u} and {key:Ctrl-R} walk along the *current branch*.
  • {key:g}{key:-} and {key:g}{key:+} walk *chronologically* — they can hop to a different branch if it was edited more recently.
  • :earlier {N}{m,s,h} jumps the buffer to its state N minutes/seconds/hours ago — across branches.
  • :later does the opposite.
  • If you have `'undofile'` set, the undo tree is saved to disk between sessions. Reopen the file tomorrow and you can still undo today's edits. (`'undofile'` and `'undodir'` are options — the option system itself is covered in Part 17.)

Full treatment in `survival.undo-tree`. For now: you literally cannot lose work in Vim. If you make a change you regret, {key:u}. If you regret the undo, {key:Ctrl-R}. If hours have passed and you don't even remember the right sequence of presses, :earlier {key:1}{key:0}{key:m} ({key:Enter}).
{key:U} (capital) is a less-common command: it undoes *all changes on the current line* since you last moved off it. Useful occasionally; the regular {key:u} is what you'll use 99% of the time.

rows: Undo all changes on the current line

blocks.title: Undo units are command-shaped, not keystroke-shaped
blocks.text: The undo system batches edits at command boundaries, not per keystroke. One normal-mode command — including a complete insert session from {key:i} through Escape — collapses into a single undo unit. That's why hitting {key:u} after typing a paragraph rips out the whole paragraph at once: the editor only inserted one undo boundary, at the moment you returned to *normal mode*. Internally undo is a tree of state snapshots; redo is a forward walk through that tree.
